whatItIs: Apple's background-activity scheduler: decides when deferrable system work (App Store updates, backups, analytics uploads) should run based on power, thermal, and usage predictions.
whenHotImplies: Almost always a wedged scheduling loop, frequently thrashing over a stuck task it keeps trying to schedule (check appstoreagent). Sustained CPU or multi-GB RSS is never normal for a scheduler. On macOS 27 betas specifically, multi-GB RSS growth is a known BiomeAgent leak: BiomeAgent re-registers an Apple Intelligence task (com.apple.intelligenceplatform.IntelligencePlatformCore.ViewLive) in a loop, and dasd accumulates memory tracking it — reaching tens of GB.
safeAction: kill — launchd respawns it fresh within seconds, reclaiming the leaked memory immediately
worstCase: A deferred background task (backup, update check) runs later than it otherwise would.

whatItIs: Part of Biome, Apple's on-device stream store for behavioral and contextual signals that feed Siri Suggestions and Apple Intelligence. BiomeAgent registers and coordinates background tasks that process these signals.
whenHotImplies: On macOS 27 betas, a known defect makes BiomeAgent re-register the same Apple Intelligence task (com.apple.intelligenceplatform.IntelligencePlatformCore.ViewLive) in a loop; the visible symptom is usually dasd's memory ballooning to tens of GB rather than BiomeAgent itself being hot. Reported to Apple as a beta bug.
safeAction: kill — respawns; killing dasd is what actually reclaims the leaked memory. If it recurs on a beta, the deeper (Siri/Apple-Intelligence-affecting) workaround is to disable BiomeAgent — reversible, and worth undoing once Apple fixes it.
worstCase: Siri Suggestions and some Apple Intelligence context may briefly stop updating until it respawns.

whatItIs: The MySQL (or Percona) database server. Hosts databases for local development and services; commonly installed via Homebrew, Docker, or a native package.
ownedBy: You (a user-run service, often started by Homebrew services or a launch agent)
whenHotImplies: High CPU is almost always a CLIENT hammering the database, not mysqld itself: a busy-polling job queue (Laravel/Sidekiq workers with a near-zero sleep), an N+1 query storm, a missing index, or a runaway migration. A telling signature is thousands of individually sub-millisecond queries per second — each looks idle in SHOW PROCESSLIST, but the aggregate rate pins a core (death by a million paper cuts). Find the client; check SHOW FULL PROCESSLIST and your app's queue workers.
worstCase: Do NOT kill it to quiet the CPU — you would drop every app's database connections and can lose in-flight writes, and the load returns the moment the client reconnects. Fix the client causing the load instead (e.g. a queue worker launched with --sleep=0).

whatItIs: The MariaDB database server (a MySQL fork). Same role and behavior as mysqld for local development and services.
whenHotImplies: As with mysqld: high CPU is almost always a client hammering it — busy-polling queue workers, N+1 queries, missing indexes, or a runaway query. The fix is on the client side.
worstCase: Killing it drops all database connections and risks in-flight writes; the load returns when the client reconnects. Fix the client, not the server.

whatItIs: A PostgreSQL backend process. Postgres runs one process per connection plus background workers (autovacuum, checkpointer, WAL writer), so several 'postgres' processes are normal.
whenHotImplies: A hot backend usually means one expensive query or a client connection doing heavy work; a hot autovacuum worker means a large table is being vacuumed. Identify the query via pg_stat_activity rather than killing the process.
worstCase: Killing a backend drops that client's transaction; killing the postmaster takes the whole database down. Diagnose the query instead.

whatItIs: An in-memory key-value store used for caching, queues, and sessions. Single-threaded for command execution.
whenHotImplies: Sustained CPU means a client is issuing expensive commands (KEYS on a large keyspace, big sorts, hot Lua scripts) or an extremely high command rate. Growing memory can mean an unbounded key set with no eviction policy. Check the slow log and MONITOR briefly.
worstCase: Killing it drops all cached data and any queue not persisted; apps relying on it will error until it restarts. Fix the client's command pattern.

whatItIs: The MongoDB database server.
whenHotImplies: High CPU usually means an unindexed query doing a collection scan, or a heavy aggregation pipeline. Check db.currentOp() and slow-query logs for the offending operation.
worstCase: Killing it drops connections and risks in-flight writes. Diagnose the query, don't kill the server.

whatItIs: A local large-language-model server that runs open models on your machine.
whenHotImplies: High CPU/GPU and large memory are EXPECTED while a model is loaded and generating — this is the workload, not a runaway. It's only pathological if it stays pegged long after you stopped using it (a stuck request) or memory never releases between runs.
safeAction: if it's stuck idle-but-hot, quitting Ollama is safe; it reloads the model on the next request
worstCase: You interrupt an in-progress generation; nothing is lost beyond that request.

whatItIs: The Docker Desktop virtual-machine backend that runs your containers on macOS. Container CPU and memory show up here because they run inside its VM.
whenHotImplies: Sustained CPU is usually a container inside it working hard (a build, a busy service, a filesystem-watch loop over a bind mount). Inspect with `docker stats` to find the container; the backend itself is rarely the culprit.
safeAction: prefer stopping the specific hot container (docker stop) over quitting Docker Desktop
worstCase: Quitting Docker stops every running container at once.

whatItIs: Handles App Store background operations: update checks, downloads, receipt validation.
whenHotImplies: A stuck background update or download retry loop. Frequently the task dasd is thrashing over when both are hot.
safeAction: kill — respawns on demand; interrupted downloads resume
worstCase: An app update arrives on the next check instead of now.

whatItIs: Spotlight's index-writing worker. Reads changed files and updates the search index.
whenHotImplies: Heavy indexing after large file churn (build trees, mail imports, migrations) — often legitimate but long-running. Persistent heat with no file churn suggests a corrupt index.
safeAction: usually wait; if persistent, rebuild the index for the hot volume (sudo mdutil -E)
worstCase: Interrupting mid-index restarts that volume's indexing from scratch.

whatItIs: The kernel itself. SPECIAL CASE: high kernel_task CPU is macOS deliberately occupying cores to force thermal throttling — it is the SYMPTOM, not the runaway.
ownedBy: Apple (the kernel — cannot and must not be killed)
whenHotImplies: Your Mac is hot. The correct diagnosis names WHO is heating it (look at the other anomalies in this window), plus airflow/charger-side factors.
worstCase: Not applicable — unkillable by design.

whatItIs: Analyzes photos and videos on-device: faces, objects, scenes, OCR for Photos search and Visual Look Up.
whenHotImplies: Working through a large or newly imported photo library, usually on AC power overnight. Legitimate but can run hot for hours; runaway if it persists for days on an unchanged library.
safeAction: kill — respawns and resumes analysis later
worstCase: Photos search/People albums update later.

whatItIs: Performs Time Machine backups.
whenHotImplies: A large backup in progress (first backup, big churn) — usually legitimate. Sustained heat with tiny backups suggests a snapshot or destination problem.
safeAction: prefer stopping the backup from Time Machine settings over killing
worstCase: Killing mid-backup can leave an incomplete snapshot to clean up (handled automatically, but the backup restarts).

whatItIs: Syncs iCloud Drive and Desktop & Documents folders.
whenHotImplies: Large sync in progress, or a sync conflict loop (same file re-uploading endlessly — check console for repeated items).
safeAction: kill — respawns and resumes sync
worstCase: Sync pauses briefly; no data loss (server is source of truth).

whatItIs: Composites every window on screen. All drawing flows through it.
whenHotImplies: Some app is forcing constant redraws (animating dashboards, broken transparency), or too many high-refresh displays. WindowServer heat is almost always ANOTHER app's fault.
worstCase: Killing it logs out the entire session instantly, losing unsaved work everywhere.

whatItIs: Enforces Gatekeeper: notarization checks, first-launch app assessment, executable policy.
whenHotImplies: Assessing a large newly-installed app bundle, or repeatedly re-assessing unsigned binaries (common with large dev trees).
worstCase: Security assessment is not something to kill; wait it out.

whatItIs: Certificate trust evaluation for TLS and code signing across the system.
whenHotImplies: A burst of trust evaluations — often an app opening many TLS connections, or revocation-check storms on a bad network.
worstCase: System-critical; heat is a symptom of a chatty client, not trustd.

whatItIs: The core of Spotlight. Coordinates indexing, spawns the mdworker processes, and answers search queries against the metadata index.
whenHotImplies: Managing a large indexing job, or churning because a volume's index is corrupt and it keeps re-dispatching work. Its worker children (mdworker/mdworker_shared) usually show the actual CPU.
safeAction: prefer mdutil over killing; if the index is corrupt, rebuild with sudo mdutil -E on the hot volume
worstCase: Killing disrupts in-flight indexing and search briefly; it respawns.

whatItIs: A short-lived Spotlight worker that opens changed files and runs the matching importer plugin to extract searchable text and metadata. Shared, sandboxed instances handle many file types.
whenHotImplies: Actively indexing new or changed files — usually a large import, download, or build tree. A single mdworker_shared pinned for a long time often means one pathological file its importer chokes on (a huge or malformed document).
safeAction: kill — mds respawns workers on demand; that file is re-indexed later
worstCase: One file's metadata is extracted a bit later; search stays otherwise intact.

whatItIs: The Spotlight worker process that extracts metadata and text from files for the search index. The non-shared form runs importers that need their own process.
whenHotImplies: Same as its shared sibling: heavy file churn being indexed, or a single file whose importer is stuck. Many mdworkers at once is normal during a big import.
safeAction: kill — mds respawns it; the file is re-indexed later
worstCase: One file's metadata lands in the index slightly later.

whatItIs: Indexes content that apps donate to Spotlight through the Core Spotlight API — Mail messages, Messages, Notes, and third-party app items — as opposed to files on disk.
whenHotImplies: An app is donating or reindexing a large batch of items (a big Mail account resync, a Notes migration). Persistent heat can mean a reindex loop over an app's data store.
safeAction: usually wait; it recovers once the donating app finishes or after an index rebuild
worstCase: In-app Spotlight results (Mail, Notes) update later; on-disk file search is unaffected.

whatItIs: Records a log of directory changes so services like Spotlight and Time Machine can learn what changed without rescanning the whole disk.
whenHotImplies: A flood of filesystem changes — a giant build, a bulk copy, or a runaway process writing endlessly. fseventsd is rarely the culprit; it is reflecting whoever is churning the disk.
worstCase: System-critical for change tracking; killing it can make Spotlight and Time Machine miss changes and force full rescans.

whatItIs: Runs the Photos library's background analysis — face recognition, scene and object detection, and building the People and Memories features — while Photos is not in the foreground.
whenHotImplies: Working through a large or freshly imported library, typically when idle and on power. Normal for hours after a big import; suspicious if it runs for days on an unchanged library.
safeAction: kill — respawns and resumes analysis later, usually overnight on power
worstCase: People albums, Memories, and photo search update later.

whatItIs: Manages the Photos library database — imports, edits, album membership, and iCloud Photos sync bookkeeping.
whenHotImplies: A large import, edit, or iCloud Photos sync in progress. Sustained heat with no library activity can indicate a database that needs repair.
safeAction: quit Photos and let it settle rather than killing; the library database is best not interrupted mid-write
worstCase: Interrupting a library write can require Photos to repair the library on next launch.

whatItIs: The system audio server. Every sound in and out of the Mac — playback, recording, device switching — flows through it.
whenHotImplies: Almost always an app hammering the audio stack (a stuck stream, a plug-in in a tight loop, constant sample-rate switching) rather than coreaudiod itself. Look for the audio app that is actually busy.
worstCase: System audio infrastructure; killing cuts all audio and can wedge apps holding audio sessions.

whatItIs: Syncs CloudKit data for iCloud and for apps that store data in iCloud — the transport behind iCloud-backed app databases.
whenHotImplies: A large CloudKit sync, or a push-driven retry storm when an app's container keeps failing to reconcile. Often hot alongside the app whose data it is syncing.
safeAction: kill — respawns and resumes sync; the server holds the authoritative copy
worstCase: iCloud-backed app data syncs a little later; no local data loss.

whatItIs: Coordinates File Provider extensions — the on-demand file system behind iCloud Drive and third-party clients like Dropbox, OneDrive, and Google Drive.
whenHotImplies: Materializing or evicting many files, or a third-party provider extension misbehaving in a sync loop. Check which provider (which cloud app) is active.
safeAction: prefer pausing the offending cloud client; killing can interrupt in-flight file materialization
worstCase: A file being downloaded on demand has to restart; providers generally recover.

whatItIs: Performs background downloads and uploads on behalf of the system and apps — the transfer engine many services hand their traffic to so it continues when the app isn't running.
whenHotImplies: A large background transfer, or a client that keeps re-queuing a failing download. The heat is bandwidth and retry work for whichever service owns the transfer.
safeAction: kill — respawns; background transfers resume from where they left off
worstCase: An in-progress download or upload pauses briefly, then resumes.

whatItIs: Handles account sync for Exchange and other server accounts — Mail, Contacts, and Calendar over ActiveSync/CardDAV/CalDAV.
whenHotImplies: A big mailbox resync, or a sync loop against a server that keeps returning errors (a classic: one item the server and Mac disagree on). Persistent heat usually points at one bad account.
safeAction: kill — respawns and resumes; the mail server holds the authoritative copy
worstCase: Mail/Contacts/Calendar sync pauses briefly for that account.

whatItIs: Syncs calendars and manages calendar accounts (iCloud and CalDAV), fetching and pushing events in the background.
whenHotImplies: A large calendar resync, or a repeating-event/invitation loop where one malformed event keeps failing to reconcile with the server.
safeAction: kill — respawns and resumes; the server holds the authoritative copy
worstCase: Calendar changes sync a little later.

whatItIs: Backs Apple Maps: fetches map tiles, geocoding, and routing data, and caches them for the Maps app and location-aware features.
whenHotImplies: Active Maps use downloading tiles and routes. Idle heat is unusual and can mean a cache or download it keeps retrying.
safeAction: kill — respawns; Maps reloads data as needed
worstCase: Maps briefly re-fetches tiles or route data.

whatItIs: Downloads and manages on-demand Apple assets — Siri voices, dictionaries, fonts, keyboard and ML models, and other 'MobileAsset' bundles delivered outside the main OS update.
whenHotImplies: Fetching a batch of assets after a login or OS update, or retrying a download that keeps failing. Often paired with a background download service.
safeAction: kill — respawns and resumes; asset downloads retry later
worstCase: An optional asset (a voice, a dictionary) arrives later.

whatItIs: Powers proactive Siri Suggestions — learning from your Mail, Contacts, and app usage to surface names, events, and links, and to detect data like addresses in text.
whenHotImplies: Processing a burst of new content (a big Mail import, lots of new messages) to update suggestions. Sustained heat can mean it is chewing on a large or problematic data set.
safeAction: kill — respawns and resumes learning later
worstCase: Siri Suggestions refresh a bit later.

whatItIs: Builds the on-device knowledge graph from your data — linking people, places, and events mined from Mail, Messages, and Contacts to feed Siri and Spotlight suggestions.
whenHotImplies: Ingesting a large batch of new personal data (a fresh Mail/Messages sync) to rebuild the graph. Usually settles once caught up; long heat can mean it keeps reprocessing the same data.
safeAction: kill — respawns and resumes; the graph rebuilds incrementally
worstCase: Suggestions and Spotlight relevance update later.

whatItIs: Collects and models usage patterns — app launches, activity, and context — to drive app predictions, Handoff, and proactive suggestions, and to inform the activity scheduler.
whenHotImplies: Processing accumulated activity data. Persistent heat can indicate a bloated or wedged CoreDuet store it keeps re-scanning.
safeAction: kill — respawns and resumes; predictions rebuild over time
worstCase: App predictions and proactive suggestions are briefly less accurate.

whatItIs: Part of Biome, Apple's on-device stream of user-activity events used by Siri and proactive features. biomesyncd syncs that data across your signed-in devices.
whenHotImplies: Reconciling a backlog of activity events across devices, or a sync loop when the Biome store on one device is out of step. A known intermittent CPU offender after setup or restore.
safeAction: kill — respawns and resumes sync
worstCase: Cross-device activity data syncs later; no user-visible data loss.

whatItIs: Runs Continuity sharing features — AirDrop, Handoff, Universal Clipboard, Instant Hotspot, and shared-media discovery.
whenHotImplies: Active discovery over Wi-Fi/Bluetooth (an AirDrop window open, nearby devices advertising), or a discovery loop when Bluetooth/Wi-Fi state keeps flapping.
safeAction: kill — respawns; sharing features re-advertise
worstCase: AirDrop/Handoff drop for a moment, then recover.

whatItIs: Enables Continuity connectivity between your Apple devices — the BLE/Wi-Fi link behind Handoff, iPhone Cellular Calls, Instant Hotspot, and Sidecar.
whenHotImplies: Constant discovery/advertising to nearby devices. Sustained heat often tracks Bluetooth or Wi-Fi churn, or a nearby device repeatedly connecting and dropping.
safeAction: kill — respawns and re-establishes Continuity links
worstCase: Handoff and cross-device calling blink out briefly.

whatItIs: Manages Apple's identity layer for iMessage, FaceTime, and Continuity — device registration and the encryption keys those services use.
whenHotImplies: Re-registering identities or reconciling accounts, often after a sign-in change or network flap. Chronic heat can mean an account stuck retrying registration.
safeAction: prefer signing the affected account out/in; killing disrupts Messages/FaceTime session state
worstCase: iMessage and FaceTime briefly lose registration and re-establish it.

whatItIs: The iMessage engine — sends and receives Messages and maintains their connection state.
whenHotImplies: Processing a large Messages backlog or attachment sync, or a send/receive retry loop against a struggling account.
safeAction: prefer quitting Messages; killing imagent drops in-flight sends and connection state
worstCase: A message in the middle of sending may need resending; history is safe.

whatItIs: Tracks call state for FaceTime and Continuity phone calls — routing, and relaying iPhone calls to the Mac.
whenHotImplies: Managing an active or ringing call, or a relay loop when the paired iPhone connection is unstable. Idle heat is unusual.
safeAction: prefer ending the call normally; killing can drop an in-progress call
worstCase: An active FaceTime or relayed phone call is dropped.

whatItIs: The FaceTime audio/video engine — captures, encodes, and streams the media for FaceTime calls.
whenHotImplies: An active FaceTime call — high CPU here is expected during a call. Heat with no call running is the anomaly and suggests a call that failed to tear down.
safeAction: prefer ending the FaceTime call; killing drops the call immediately
worstCase: The active FaceTime call ends abruptly.

whatItIs: Apple's built-in malware scanner. Runs periodic background scans (each scan module has its own XProtectRemediator process) to detect and remove known malware families.
whenHotImplies: A scheduled malware scan in progress — legitimate and periodic, and can briefly use real CPU and disk. Suspicious only if it runs continuously rather than in short bursts.
safeAction: prefer letting the scan finish; it is harmless and reschedules if killed, but it is doing real security work
worstCase: A malware scan is skipped and simply runs again on the next schedule.

whatItIs: Manages macOS software updates — checking for, downloading, and preparing system updates.
whenHotImplies: An update check, download, or 'preparing' phase underway. Sustained heat can mean a download that keeps restarting or an update stuck preparing.
safeAction: prefer managing it from System Settings > General > Software Update; killing interrupts the download/prepare, which resumes
worstCase: An in-progress update download or preparation restarts.

whatItIs: Enforces Transparency, Consent, and Control — the privacy permissions for Camera, Microphone, Screen Recording, Files, and Accessibility, including the consent prompts.
whenHotImplies: A burst of permission checks, often an app repeatedly probing an access it lacks. tccd is enforcing, not the runaway — find the app triggering the checks.
worstCase: Privacy-critical; killing it can break permission prompts and leave apps unable to gain or verify access.

whatItIs: Validates code signatures when executables and libraries load — a core part of system integrity and Gatekeeper enforcement.
whenHotImplies: A wave of first-time code-signature validations — launching many new binaries at once, or a large unsigned/ad-hoc-signed dev tree. It is verifying whoever is launching, not looping on its own.
worstCase: Security-critical; killing it can block app launches and destabilize the system.

whatItIs: Performs app and package installation work in the background.
whenHotImplies: An install or update actively unpacking and writing files. Sustained heat can mean an install stuck retrying or working through a very large package.
worstCase: Killing mid-install can leave a partially installed app that must be reinstalled — do not interrupt.

whatItIs: Manages sleep, wake, display dimming, and power assertions — the arbiter of when the Mac may idle-sleep.
whenHotImplies: Rarely hot itself; when it is, some component is thrashing power assertions (repeatedly preventing and allowing sleep). The fix is the assertion holder, not powerd.
worstCase: System-critical; killing it disrupts sleep/wake and power management.

whatItIs: The system watchdog — monitors for hung critical processes and can force a restart if the system stops responding.
whenHotImplies: Elevated activity can mean it is tracking a process that is hanging or missing its checkpoints. It is the watcher, not the runaway.
worstCase: System-critical; never kill — it guards system responsiveness.

whatItIs: Coordinates mounting and unmounting of disks and volumes, and notifies apps when storage appears or disappears.
whenHotImplies: A storm of mount/unmount events — a flaky external drive reconnecting, or disk images repeatedly attaching. It is reacting to the churn, not causing it.
worstCase: System-critical for volume management; killing it can wedge mounting and eject handling.

whatItIs: The lightweight system notification hub — delivers named state-change signals between processes via the notify(3) API. Used constantly by the whole system.
whenHotImplies: A process posting or observing notifications in a tight loop, flooding the hub. The heat is a chatty client; notifyd is just the switchboard.
worstCase: Extremely central; killing it destabilizes many processes that rely on notifications.

whatItIs: Reads and writes preferences (.plist / NSUserDefaults) on behalf of apps and the system, serializing access to each preference domain.
whenHotImplies: An app hammering its preferences — writing defaults in a loop. cfprefsd is doing the I/O for a misbehaving client; identify the app churning its settings.
worstCase: Killing it during a write risks losing or corrupting preferences for apps writing at that moment.

whatItIs: Resolves users, groups, and authentication — local accounts and any network directory (LDAP/Active Directory) the Mac is bound to.
whenHotImplies: A flood of identity lookups or auth attempts, or slow/failing queries against a bound network directory. On networked Macs, a slow directory server is a common cause.
worstCase: Security- and login-critical; killing it can break authentication and account lookups.

whatItIs: The Human Interface Device daemon — processes input events from keyboards, mice, trackpads, and other HID devices.
whenHotImplies: A device generating a storm of events — a stuck key, a jittery mouse or trackpad, or a misbehaving HID peripheral. The device is the cause, not hidd.
worstCase: Input-critical; killing it can leave the Mac unresponsive to keyboard and pointer.

whatItIs: Collects and stores the unified system log that every process writes to, and serves it to tools like Console and the log command.
whenHotImplies: Some process is logging at a furious rate, flooding the log stream. logd's heat is the volume of a chatty component — hunt the process spamming the log.
worstCase: Killing it disrupts logging continuity for the whole system; it respawns, but log history around the gap is lost.

whatItIs: Backs Launch Services — the registry of installed apps, their document and URL type handlers, and the 'Open With' associations.
whenHotImplies: Rebuilding app registrations after installs or an OS update, or a loop re-registering a malformed app bundle. Often hot right after large app changes.
safeAction: usually wait; killing respawns it but disrupts app-launch and icon lookups briefly
worstCase: File/URL associations and app launching hiccup while it rebuilds.

whatItIs: Manages Apple ID authentication and account state — the tokens and sign-in status that iCloud and Apple services depend on.
whenHotImplies: Re-authenticating or reconciling Apple ID state, often after a password change or network flap. Chronic heat can mean an auth retry loop.
safeAction: prefer resolving the Apple ID sign-in in System Settings; killing disrupts in-flight authentication
worstCase: Apple ID-backed services briefly lose authentication and re-establish it.

whatItIs: Maintains the persistent connection to Apple Push Notification service and fans incoming pushes out to apps and system services (Mail, Messages, iCloud).
whenHotImplies: A reconnect loop on a flaky network, or a flood of incoming pushes. Sustained heat often tracks network instability rather than apsd itself.
safeAction: prefer waiting or toggling the network; killing drops the push connection, delaying notifications until it reconnects
worstCase: Push notifications (Messages, Mail, iCloud) are delayed briefly while the connection re-establishes.

whatItIs: Generates a crash report when an app or process crashes, capturing the stack for diagnostics.
whenHotImplies: Something is crashing repeatedly — ReportCrash spikes each time it writes a report. Constant activity means an app is crash-looping; the fix is that app, not ReportCrash.
safeAction: kill — harmless; it is invoked again on the next crash
worstCase: One crash report is not written; the crashing app is the real issue to address.

whatItIs: Samples unresponsive processes to produce a 'spindump' report the system uses to diagnose hangs and beachballs.
whenHotImplies: The system is sampling a process that is hanging — spindump running means something else is stuck (a beachballing app). It is the diagnostician, not the culprit.
safeAction: kill — harmless; it runs again the next time a process hangs
worstCase: One hang report is skipped; the hanging app is the real target.
